coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of carbon dot and residual carbon nanobeads: Implications for ion sensing, medicinal and biological applications
coredata.dc:creator: Gaddam, Rohit Ranganathan
Mukherjee, Sudip
Punugupati, Neelambaram
Vasudevan, D
Patra, Chitta Ranjan
Narayan, Ramanuj
VSN Kothapalli, Raju
coredata.dc:description: Synthesis of carbon dots (Cdots) via chemical route involves disintegration of carbon materials into nano-domains, wherein, after extraction of Cdots, the remaining carbon material is discarded. The present work focuses on studying even the leftover carbon residue namely, carbon nanobeads (CNBs) as an equally important material for applications on par with that of carbon dot. It employs oxidative treatment of carbonised gum olibanum resin (GOR) to produce the carbons namely Cdots and CNBs (as the residue). The Cdots (~5–10nm) exhibit blue-green fluorescence with an optical absorption at ~300nm unlike the CNBs (40–50nm) which fail to exhibit fluorescence. The fluorescence behaviour exhibited by Cdots were utilized for heavy metal ion sensing of Pb2+, Hg2+ and Cd2+ ions in aqueous media. Interestingly, both Cdots and CNBs are biocompatible to normal cell lines but cytotoxic to cancer cell lines, observed during several in vitro experiments (cell viability assay, cell cycle assay, apoptosis assay, ROS determination assay, caspase-9 activity assay). Additionally, Cdots exhibit bright green fluorescence in B16F10 cells. The Cdots and CNB's demonstrate multifunctional activities (sensor, cellular imaging and cancer therapy) in biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Carbon nanobeads
Green chemistry
Sensing
Biocompatible
Cancer theranostics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Extraction of resin from gum-olibanum (GO) powder
Synthesis of carbon nanobeads and carbon dots
Cell viability assay using MTT reagent
Cell imaging study using fluorescence microscopy
Quantification of cellular uptake of Cdots in B16F10 and NIH-3T3 cells
Analysis of apoptosis by flow cytometry
Quantification of intracellular reactive oxygen species (DCFDA and DHE)
Structure and property studies of Cdots and CNBs
Fluorescence studies of Cdots and CNBs
Cancer therapy using Cdots and CNBs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Transmission electron microscopy of (a) CNBs and (b) Cdots respectively.

(a) Absorption spectra of Cdots, (b) Photoluminescence spectra of Cdots from 300 to 600nm excitation wavelength with equal increase in each step, (c) Fluorescence quenching by addition of 0.5 to 5μM of mercury salt in an increasing order of 0.5μM at each step (λex =320nm) and (d) Stern Volmer plot showing the dependency of fluorescence intensity on the concentration of Hg2+.

Cell imaging of Cdots (1mg/mL) treated B16F10 cell lines using fluorescence microscopy. Bright field phase images (a1,b1), fluorescence (a2,b2) and merged images (a3,b3) of Cdots (1mg·mL−1) treated B16F10-mouse melanoma cancer cells were taken at 10× and 20× microscopic object, respectively [Scale bar length: 50μm].

In vitro cell viability assay of Cdots and CNBs using MTT assay in different normal and cancer cell lines. (a,b) cell viability assay with different doses of Cdots and CNBs at 24h in normal NIH-3T3 and CHO cell lines, respectively, (c,d) cell viability assay with different doses of Cdots and CNBs at 24h in cancer B16F10 and MDA-MB-231 cell lines, respectively.
Quantification of fluorescence intensity of Cdots in B16F10 and NIH-3T3 cells using fluorescence spectrometry.

Flow cytometric analysis of B16F10 cells treated with Cdots and CNBs using Annexin V FITC and their quantification of apoptosis. (a) UT, (b) Cdots (1.3mg/mL), (c) CNBs (1.0mg/mL), (d) quantification data.

Cell cycle analysis of (a) untreated B16F10 cells, (b) cells treated Cdots (1.3mg/mL) and (c) CNBs (1.0mg/mL) for 24h. (d) Quantification of % cellular population in different cellular phase such as Sub-G1, G0/G1, S and G2/M phase using FACS.

Quantification of intracellular ROS (a & c: H2O2 and b & d: O2
−) in (a–b) B16F10 and (c–d) NIH-3T3 cells treated with Cdots (1.3mg/mL) and CNBs (1.0mg/mL) using DCFDA or DHE dyes, using spectrofluorimetry [for H2DCFDA, λEx =460nm & λEm =530nm and for DHE, λEx =540nm & λEm =600nm].
Caspase 9 activity assay in B16F10 cells treated with Cdots (1.3mg/mL) and CNBs (1.0mg/mL) for 24h using colorimetric assay.
Schematic representation of gum mediated synthesis of carbon dot and carbon nanobeads and their multifunctional applications in ion sensing, bio-imaging and anticancer activity.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of carbon dot and residual carbon nanobeads: Implications for ion sensing, medicinal and biological applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymers and Functional Materials Division, CSIR-Indian Institute of Chemical Technology, Hyderabad 500007, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biomaterials Group, CSIR-Indian Institute of Chemical Technology, Hyderabad 500007, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Electrodics and Electrocatalysis Division, CSIR-Central Electrochemical Research Institute, Karaikudi 630006, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Training and Development Complex, CSIR Campus, CSIR Road, Taramani, Chennai 600 113, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors have equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of carbon dots (Cdots) via chemical route involves disintegration of carbon materials into nano-domains, wherein, after extraction of Cdots, the remaining carbon material is discarded. The present work focuses on studying even the leftover carbon residue namely, carbon nanobeads (CNBs) as an equally important material for applications on par with that of carbon dot. It employs oxidative treatment of carbonised gum olibanum resin (GOR) to produce the carbons namely Cdots and CNBs (as the residue). The Cdots (~5–10nm) exhibit blue-green fluorescence with an optical absorption at ~300nm unlike the CNBs (40–50nm) which fail to exhibit fluorescence. The fluorescence behaviour exhibited by Cdots were utilized for heavy metal ion sensing of Pb2+, Hg2+ and Cd2+ ions in aqueous media. Interestingly, both Cdots and CNBs are biocompatible to normal cell lines but cytotoxic to cancer cell lines, observed during several in vitro experiments (cell viability assay, cell cycle assay, apoptosis assay, ROS determination assay, caspase-9 activity assay). Additionally, Cdots exhibit bright green fluorescence in B16F10 cells. The Cdots and CNB's demonstrate multifunctional activities (sensor, cellular imaging and cancer therapy) in biomedical applications.

Gum olibanum resin mediated green synthesis of carbon dots (Cdots) and carbon nanobeads (CNBs) can be used towards multifunctional applications in ion sensing, bio-imaging, drug delivery and anti-cancer activities.
Image 2

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon, due its exceptional thermal, mechanical and electrical properties, serves as interesting and promising material for material scientists [1–3]. The never-ending search for better materials with superior properties has catered the development of several new carbon allotropes unknown before. The present research mainly focuses on obtaining materials that can add additional functionalities to carbon nanomaterials like fluorescence, water solubility or high conductivity depending on the end application [4]. As a result, application specific synthesis or modification to carbon nanomaterial is employed so as to compensate for the deficient property in the original material. Amongst many carbon nanomaterials developed, Cdots are a relatively new addition, which have secured a significant place in the carbon family [5]. They are superior to semi-conductor quantum dots due to having low toxicity, better biocompatibility and chemical inertness [6–11]. They are also easy to synthesize, cost effective, resistant to photo-bleaching and exhibit tunable size-dependent luminescence. Many methods have been proposed for the synthesis of Cdots including laser ablation of graphene, electrochemical treatment of graphite, thermal oxidation of molecular precursors, proton beam irradiation of nanodiamond, acid treatment on candle soot and oxidative treatments on camphor soot [2,3,8–17]. They exhibit potential applications in bio-imaging, solar cells and medical diagnostics. Further, several reports are available wherein Cdots are utilized for drug delivery or as theranostics agents for different biomedical applications [8,10,11, 13,14,18,19].

The synthesis of Cdots via chemical routes involves disintegration of carbon materials into particles of smaller domains. Once Cdots are obtained the remaining carbon material is discarded. It is innovative of the present study to consider the left over carbon as a useful resource and utilise it for biological and medicinal applications. In this regard the present work analyses the CNBs which are obtained after the filtration of carbon dots. The CNBs are black in colour and quasi spherical. Their size ranges from ~40–50nm. They do not exhibit fluorescence. The π-π interactions between the mutual carbon nanoparticles make them stack like a group of beads attached together. Sharon et al. has reported CNBs formation from camphor via pyrolysis, suitable for energy applications [20]. In another report, synthesis of carbon nanoparticles from camphor has been reported by Gaddam et al. [1]. These nanobeads exhibit wide range of applications in electronics, coatings, textile, solar cells, batteries, drug delivery, bio-imaging etc. [12] The CNBs and Cdots are generally reported to be synthesized individually under different conditions. However, the present work reports simultaneous synthesis of CNBs and Cdots from carbonised gum olibanum resin (GOR) as the materials were obtained from the same precursor by a simple filtration step to separate them and no loss of compound elsewhere were observed. The properties of the obtained carbon materials are dependent on the geometry and surface functional groups. Here, the starting material is bio-sourced polymeric gum extruded from the incense tree Burseraceae [21]. In the classical Indian medicine it is used as an anti-inflammatory remedy and several recent studies have shown a positive impact of the gum in rheumatism and anti-cancer activity [22]. From the standpoint of sustainability, carbon materials derived from gum olibanum eradicates the reliance on fossil fuel precursors for material fabrication. It would be worthwhile to mention that this is the first report on the simultaneous preparation of carbon dot and carbon nanobeads from GOR.

In the current report GOR is initially prepared from GO and then carbonised. The obtained product is treated with piranha solution. The ash of the gum is treated with oxidising agents and filtered out to obtain Cdots and CNBs. The formations of these materials are confirmed from microscopic and spectroscopic analysis. The fluorescent properties were studied and the effect of Cdots in sensing heavy metal ions was investigated. Further, we checked the in vitro cytotoxicity of both CNBs and Cdots in different normal and cancer cells. Both CNBs and Cdots show excellent biocompatibility in normal cells. However, they exhibit cytotoxicity in cancer cells. Furthermore, Cdots exhibit bright green fluorescence inside live cells, which can be utilized for live cell imaging. The results altogether suggest that Cdots demonstrate multifunctional activities (sensor, cellular imaging, and cancer therapy) in biomedical applications.

Gum olibanum (GO) was obtained from the local market. Sulphuric acid (98%), 30% hydrogen peroxide solution (AR), Lead acetate, Mercuric chloride (AR grade, 99.5% pure) and Cadmium Chloride (AR grade, 99.9% pure) were purchased from SD Fine chemicals, Mumbai. All the chemicals were used as received without any purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of resin from gum-olibanum (GO) powder
ce:section.ce:para: 12g of GO was powdered and solubilised in hexane. The solubilised material was placed in a round bottom flask and stirred under inert atmosphere for 48h. Subsequently, the mixture was filtered out and the hexane from the mixture evaporated under reduced pressure. A highly viscous honey coloured material was initially obtained which was immediately transferred onto a Teflon dish and allowed to cool. It was then dried for 28h in a vacuum oven. Finally, 10g of bright yellow solid coloured resin (GOR) was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon nanobeads and carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The bright yellow coloured solid GOR obtained in the previous step was then crushed and taken into a 500mL round-bottom flask and heated to around 350–400°C to ensure complete degradation of the material. The charred GOR was then refluxed with 45mL of piranha solution (an oxidative mixture of sulphuric acid and hydrogen peroxide in 7:3 ratio) for 5h. Subsequently, the mixture was diluted with deionised water and filtered using a Whatman filter paper, Grade 93 with a pore size of 10μm. The CNBs remained as black coloured residue on the filter paper while the Cdots were obtained as the bright yellow filtrate. The CNBs were washed with water repeatedly, then with acetone and finally dried in vacuum oven for 48h. The Cdot solution was neutralised with sodium hydroxide which lead to the formation of salt. The salt was removed by evaporation of the dispersion and then the re-dispersion of the Cdots in N,N dimethyl formamide. Then the DMF was filtered out and the filtrate was evaporated to get pure Cdots. The Cdots were then redispersed in water for further analysis.

AR samples of mercuric chloride, cadmium chloride and lead acetate were used for studies on fluorescence quenching of carbon dots upon addition of these metal ions. A 0.1M stock solution of each of these substrates was prepared. 2mL solution of Cdots was taken in the cuvette for fluorescence measurement. To this, increasing amounts of Hg2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+ (0.5, 1, 1.5mL and so on) were added as the case may be. With each addition, the solution was stirred and the fluorescence measured.

Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was recorded using Thermo Nicolet Nexus 670 spectrometer. Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed with a Horiba Fluoromax 4 spectrophotometer at different excitation wavelengths. UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu 220V (E) UV–vis spectrophotometer. Transmission electron micrographs (TEM) were recorded on a JEOL JEM-100CX electron microscope. The fluorescence intensity of biological samples was measured using spectrofluorometry (Biotek Synergy).

All cell lines [NIH-3T3: mouse fibroblast cell lines, CHO: Chinese hamster ovarian cell lines, B16F10: murine melanoma cancer cell lines and MDA-MB-231: human breast epithelial cancer cell lines] were purchased from ATCC. All normal and cancer cell lines were cultured in DMEM (Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium) media supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin-streptomycin antibiotics (50mg each/Lt) and 5% l-glutamine, in a humidified 5% CO2 incubator at 37°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability assay using MTT reagent
ce:section.ce:para: All samples were kept under UV irradiation for 15–20min before doing in vitro experiments. Cells (1×104) were seeded in 96 well plates and kept for 24h in the CO2 incubator to grow. Cell viability assay was carried out by incubating NIH-3T3, CHO, B16F10 and MDA-MB-231 cells using Cdots and CNBs in a dose dependent manner (Cdots: 130–1300μg·mL−1; CNBs: 100–1000μg·mL−1) for 24h using MTT reagents according to already published protocol [21,23]. Results were calculated and expressed as percent of cell viability=([A570 (treated cells)−background]/[A570 (untreated cells)−background]) ×100.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging study using fluorescence microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: B16F10 cells (2×104) were seeded in 24 well plates and kept for 24h to grow in the CO2 incubator. These cells were incubated with Cdots (1mg·mL−1) for 12h. All the treated cells and control untreated cells were extensively washed by phosphate buffer saline (PBS; pH=7.4) for 4 times and kept in Hank's Balanced Salt Solution (HBSS buffer; pH=7.4). Finally, the fluorescence images were monitored and captured in a fluorescence microscope (Nikon Eclipse TE2000-E). The green fluorescence emission (λEm =525nm) was collected with a 10× and 20× microscope objective after excitation at λEx =420–495nm [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of cellular uptake of Cdots in B16F10 and NIH-3T3 cells
ce:section.ce:para: In order to check the cellular uptake of Cdots (1.3mg·mL−1) in cancer (B16F10) and normal cells (NIH-3T3), both cells (1×105 cells) were incubated with Cdots for overnight. After overnight incubation, the cells were washed extensively by DPBS (4–5 times) to remove the surface bound nano particles. The cells were lysed using RIPA buffer containing 1% PIC and lysate was obtained after centrifugation at 12000rpm for 15min at 4°C. Further, the protein content of those cell lysates was measured by Bradford assay. Finally, the uptake of Cdots was quantitatively measured using fluorescence spectrometry (Biotek Synergy) using the fluorescence intensity (λEx =460nm & λEm =530nm for green fluorescence) and DHE stained cells (λEx =540nm & λEm =600nm for red fluorescence). The results were expressed as normalized green fluorescence intensity per μg of protein.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of apoptosis by flow cytometry
ce:section.ce:para: B16F10 cells (4×105 cells) were seeded in 60mm dishes in DMEM complete media containing 10% FBS to grow. After 24h of incubation with Cdots (1.3mg·mL−1) and CNBs (1mg·mL−1), the cells were washed 2–3 times with DPBS. The treated and untreated cells were used for apoptosis assay using FITC Annexin V Apoptosis Detection Kit (BD Biosciences) by FACScan flow cytometer, according to standard manufacturer's protocol.

B16F10 cells (4×105 cells) were seeded in 60mm dishes in DMEM complete media containing 10% FBS to grow. After 24h of incubation with Cdots (1.3mg·mL−1) and CNBs (1mg·mL−1), the cells were washed 2–3 times with DPBS. After washing with cold DPBS, cells were fixed with ~70% ethanol for overnight. The fixed cells were again washed with cold DPBS and stained with 50μg·mL−1propidium iodide solution containing RnaseA and TritonX. Finally, the DNA content of the untreated and treated cells was measured by FACScan flow cytometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of intracellular reactive oxygen species (DCFDA and DHE)
ce:section.ce:para:
The intracellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) H2O2 and O2 – were quantified by spectrofluorometry by using DCFDA (for H2O2) and DHE (for O2
–) dyes. B16F10 cells (2×104 cells) were seeded in 6-well plate in DMEM complete media containing 10% FBS to grow. After 24h of incubation with Cdots (1.3mg·mL−1) and CNBs (1mg·mL−1), the cells were washed 2–3 times with DPBS. After washing with DPBS (3–4 times), the cells were incubated with either 30μM of H2DCFDA (30min) or 10μM of DHE (15min) in dark. For untreated B16F10 and NIH-3T3 cells, dye was not used for staining and kept as blank. The cells were again extensively washed with DPBS (3–4 times) and lysed using RIPA buffer having 1% protease inhibitor cocktail (PIC). The lysed cell suspension was then centrifuged at 12000rpm for 15min at 4°C to obtain cell lysate. The protein content of cell lysates was determined by Bradford assay using published protocol. 100μL of corresponding cell lysates [for H2DCFDA, λEx =460nm & λEm =530nm and for DHE, λEx =540nm & λEm =600nm] were used to measure the fluorescence intensity. The quantification of both H2O2 and O2
− was plotted as % normalized fluorescence intensity against per μg of protein.

B16F10 cells (4×105 cells) were seeded in 60mm dishes in DMEM complete media to grow. After 24h of incubation with Cdots (1.3mg·mL−1) and CNBs (1mg·mL−1), the cells were washed 2–3 times with DPBS. The cells were then subjected to analysis for caspase-9 activity assay using manufacturer protocol [Cat No# 218824, Caspase 9 Assay Kit, Calbiochem, EMD Chemicals, Inc].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and property studies of Cdots and CNBs
ce:section.ce:para: Gum mediated green synthesis of carbon dots and carbon nanobeads and their multifunctional applications in ion sensing, bio-imaging and anticancer activity are represented as Scheme 1 .

Gum olibanum (GO) predominantly contains boswellic acid (For structure see Fig. S1 in Supplementary information) along with other organic materials. Carbonising the GOR degrades these organic materials into carbon at high temperature. Further an oxidative treatment of the carbonised GOR with piranha solution was performed to obtain Cdots and CNBs. The piranha solution is a highly reactive mixture of sulphuric acid and hydrogen peroxide which releases nascent oxygen, which is capable of degrading carbon bonds in the GOR finally leading to the formation of Cdots and CNBs (Fig.1 ). The surface groups of the obtained carbon materials were initially analysed using FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. S2 in Supplementary information). The FTIR profile of Cdots show peaks at 3444cm−1, 2930cm−1, 1634cm−1, 1400cm−1, 1271cm−1, 1108cm−1 and 1038cm−1, indicating the presence of OH, CH, C O, NH2, COC and CO bonds, respectively. CNBs also show similar peaks. However, a peak appeared at 1716cm−1, representing the carboxylic acid groups present on the surface. Also peaks at 2920cm−1 and 2964cm−1 reveal the saturated CH stretching bands of CH3 and CH2, respectively [2,3]. The TEM profile of Cdots and CNBs show that their sizes range from ~5–12nm to ~40–50nm respectively (Fig. 1a and b, Fig. S3 in Supplementary information) [1–3]. The constituents of Cdots and CNBs were further explored using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. S4 in Supplementary information). It is seen that both CNBs and Cdots exhibit graphitic C1s peak at~284eV and O1s peak at ~532eV. Cdots additionally contain N1s peak at ~400eV, which show effective heteroatom doping within the Cdots. Both Cdots and CNBs show C1s peaks at 284.6 (C C), 283.4 (CC) and 286.3 (CO) eV respectively. Additionally, Cdots show C O peak at 288.6eV and CNBs show COO peak at 287eV [25,26]. Similar observations could also be made from the O1s spectra. Therefore, the XPS results also confirm the presence of sp2 and sp3 hybridised carbon in both the carbon nanomaterials.

Raman analysis of CNBs and Cdots show two bands namely G-band and D-band respectively (Fig. S5). The G-band or graphitic band arises due to an ideal lattice vibration mode with E2g symmetry. D-band or defect band arises due to characteristic disorder in graphite. In case of CNB a more intense G-band (1594cm−1) relative to D-band (1332cm−1) could be observed. This indicates better degree of graphitisation unlike the C-dot. In case of C-dot a sharp intense D1 peak at 1250cm−1 can be attributed to lattice vibration mode in graphite with A1g symmetry. The D3 band at 1338cm−1 originates from the amorphous fraction of carbon present in the Cdot (could be assigned to organic molecules, functional groups or fragments). The G-band respective to that of Cdot could be observed at 1522cm−1 [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence studies of Cdots and CNBs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical absorption peak of Cdots at about 300nm along with an extension that proceeds to the visible region can be observed from UV–Visible spectroscopy (Fig. 2a). This corresponds to the π-π* transition of C C and n-π* transition of C O in the nanocarbons. The fluorescence property of Cdots is the most interesting factor that paves way for further investigations. The reason for the fluorescence exhibited by Cdots is not well understood. However, theories predict that the presence of surface traps could be responsible for its inherent fluorescence. The fluorescence spectra of Cdots were observed by increasing the excitation wavelength from 300 to 500 with an interval of 20nm. An observable shift in the emission wavelengths at higher intensities is realised from Fig. 2b. The shift in the wavelength could possibly be due to the effect of Cdots size [13]. Smaller sized Cdots might get excited at lower wavelengths unlike the comparatively larger Cdots. The effect is similar to that observed in semiconducting quantum dot due to quantum confinement effect. The intensity of the emission wavelength predominantly depends on the number of Cdots excited at a particular time. A series of emission traps between π-π* of CC on the surface of Cdots may take place owing to the functional groups present on their surface. Therefore, depending on the wavelength of excitation the corresponding surface emission trap dominates [28]. The photograph of fluorescence under ultra violet light is represented in Fig. S6 in Supplementary information. A blue–green fluorescence could be observed in contrast to the initial yellow coloured sample under normal visible light.

The FTIR spectral data suggest the Cdots termination with carboxyl group. The carboxyl group possibly interacts with metal ions thus giving rise to the quenching property for ion-sensing. In general the sensing mechanism may be either static or dynamic in nature. The present Cdots were treated with the heavy metal ions of mercury, cadmium and lead to sense their quenching profile. It can be noted that the presence of such heavy metals in water medium can be toxic and eventually fatal. Especially, Hg2+ ions can easily penetrate the skin, respiratory organs and gastrointestinal tissues leading to damage of the central nervous system, mitosis impairment or even damage to the DNA. Although other methods of ion sensing like atomic absorption spectroscopy, using organic dyes or inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy do exist, the search is still on for a cost effective greener method of ion sensing. Sensing of metal ions using Cdots can be looked upon as one such method.

It is seen from the observation that micro molar concentrations of Hg2+ effectively quench the fluorescence profile (Fig. 2c). The type of fluorescence being non-dynamic in nature can very well be observed from the Stern-Volmer plot (Fig. 2d). In a static quenching a non-fluorescent complex is formed between the fluorophore and the quencher. Further studies however, need to be performed so as to confirm the type of quenching. Similar quenching profiles can also be observed in the case of Cd2+ and Pb2+. The sensing profile of Cd2+ and the Stern-Volmer graph for studying the type of fluorescence can be found in Fig. S7(a) and (b) in Supplementary information respectively. The profile is similar to that of Hg2+ sensing. Similar conclusions can be drawn on Pb2+ sensing (Fig. S6(c) and (d) in Supplementary information). From the Fig. S8 in Supplementary information it can be inferred that Cdots are more sensitive to Cd2+ than the other two heavy metal ions of interest in the present study. The sensitivity of Cd2+ metal ion could probably be due to stronger affinity towards carboxyl groups than the other two metal ions. Though many types of Cdots have been prepared, each of them sense different ions uniquely depending on the technique of preparation, the primary reason being the typical size and dimension of the material that interact with ions [2]. In the present work, each ion showed a different sensing profile in their fluorescence spectra (Fig. S8 in Supplementary information). Although this can be correlated with specificity, this study is just a preliminary one. Further investigation into the method of fluorescence quenching, specificity in sensing by anchoring moieties onto the C dots will be carried out in future. Hence such systems can be exploited in targeted sensing of ions in river water or even within biological medium once synthesis methods have been standardised. The CNBs on the other hand fail to show fluorescence.

Other biological studies were carried out with both Cdots and CNBs to find out their functional activity in biological systems. Several organic dyes as well as fluorophores (Rhodamine, FITC, BODIPY, Alexa Fluor, Cy5.5, quantum dots etc.) are well known as fluorescent staining agent for the cell imaging and biosensor studies due to their exhibiting fluorescence. Previous reports also suggest that the fluorescent carbon dots can be used as cell imaging agent [8,10–14,18,19,29–32]

The guar gum mediated as-synthesized Cdots exhibit intense green fluorescence upon excitation at λEx =420–495nm. This result encouraged us to check the fluorescence property of Cdots inside live cells. Consequently, cancer cells (B16F10) were incubated with Cdots (1mg/mL) for cell imaging studies. Fig. 3 shows the bright field (BF), green fluorescence and their merged images of Cdots in B16F10 cells. B16F10 cell lines treated with Cdots exhibit bright green fluorescence inside the cells due to presence of fluorescent Cdots; while untreated control cells did not show any green fluorescence (Fig. S9 in Supplementary information). Low concentration of Cdots (<1mg·mL−1) also show bright fluorescence properties (observed by fluorescence microscopy) in cellular level (B16F10), suggesting the live cell imaging of Cdots of cancer cell in future.

For the successful application of nanomaterials towards different biomedical applications, it is necessary to check their in vitro cytotoxicity or biocompatibility in different normal cell lines. To evaluate these properties of the Cdots and CNBs, normal cells (NIH-3T3 and CHO cell lines) were incubated with different doses of Cdots (130μg·mL−1 to 1300μg·mL−1) and CNBs (100μg·mL−1 to 1000μg·mL−1) for 24h. Results show that, Cdots do not show inhibition of cell viability (NIH-3T3 and CHO) up to the treatment concentration of 1300μg·mL−1 (Fig. 7.a), whereas CNBs do not exhibit cytotoxicity to NIH-3T3 and CHO up to 500μg·mL−1 (Fig. 7.b). The results indicate that both Cdots and CNBs are biocompatible in various normal cell lines, suggesting the theranostics application of these nanoparticles using nanomedicine approach.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cancer therapy using Cdots and CNBs
ce:section.ce:para: Materials often behave differently towards cancer cells, due to mainly, cellular membrane integrity, pH dependent toxicity and release of ions, receptor based uptake, different rate of metabolism compared to normal cells etc. [33–38] To compare the toxicity of Cdots and CNBs in normal and cancer cells, we incubate various cancer cells (B16F10 and MDA-MB-231) with different doses of Cdots (130μg·mL−1 to 1300μg·mL−1) and CNBs (100μg·mL−1 to 1000μg·mL−1) for 24h. Both, Cdots and CNBs exhibit dose dependent inhibition of cancer cells (Fig. 4 .c–d). We further calculated the degree of inhibition of cancer cells by the determination of IC50 (50% cell killing) value of Cdots and CNBs in both the cancer cells and the results are tabulated in SI·Table 1. Results clearly show that IC50 values of CNBs are much lower compared to IC50 values of Cdots in both the cancer cells, indicating the higher anticancer efficacy of CNBs (SI·Table 1). However, under similar conditions these nanoparticles are not cytotoxic in normal cells. It is surprising that these materials are biocompatible in normal cells but cytotoxic in cancerous cells under similar experimental conditions. Hence, these results suggest that these nanoparticles could be useful as anticancer agents.

The quantification of the green fluorescence intensity of cell lysate (μg of protein) obtained after the incubation of cells (B16F10 and NIH-3T3) treated with Cdots was carried out. The cell lysate was collected after extensive washing of cells with PBS. The results demonstrate that fluorescence intensity of Cdots in cancer cells (B16F10) is higher than that of untreated B16F10 as well as NIH-3T3 cells treated with Cdots (Fig. 5 ). The results indicate that cancer cells show greater uptake of Cdots compared to normal (NIH-3T3) cell. The results are further supported by in vitro cytotoxicity data (Fig. 4.a–d). Similarly, we predict that enhanced cytotoxicity of CNBs in cancer cells compared to normal cells is probably due to higher uptake of CNBs in cancer cells than that of normal cells.

B16F10 is an aggressive murine melanoma cancer cell lines that rapidly grows with severe malignancy. It is to be noted that malignant melanoma is the sixth most frequent cancer in the U.S., with an approximate 48,000 deaths worldwide per year [39,40]. Poor early diagnosis and higher rate of malignancy increases the mortality. Our in vitro data supports that Cdots and CNBs are more cytotoxic towards B16F10 cells compared to other cancers cells (e.g. MDA-MB-231) (Fig. 4c–d). Considering these issues, we selected B16F10 cancer cells as model cancer cells for detailed mechanistic study using Cdots and CNBs.

Apoptosis or programmed cancer cell death is one of the vital mechanisms for anticancer activity of any drug/nanomaterial [41]. To test the extent of apoptosis; we carried out apoptosis assay by FITC Annexin-V staining of B16F10 cells treated with Cdots (1.3mg·mL−1) and CNBs (1mg·mL−1). The results demonstrate that cells treated with Cdots and CNBs shows ~9.5% and ~20.2% apoptosis, respectively, whereas an untreated control cell shows ~3.5% apoptosis (Fig. 6 .a-d). Results together indicate that cancer cells treated with Cdots and CNBs show more apoptosis as well as more anticancer activity.

Anticancer nanomaterials can induce cell cycle arrest, in any of the four phases namely sub-G1, G0/G1, S and G2/M [42]. Cell cycle results show considerable shift (~11%) in G2/M phase of B16F10 cells treated with Cdots (1.3mg·mL−1) compared to untreated cells. The results indicate G2/M cellular arrest (Fig. 7 .a–b, d). On the other hand, cell population increase (~18%) in sub-G1 phase of B16F10 cells treated with in CNBs (1mg·mL−1) compared to untreated B16F10 cells, indicating sub-G1 phase arrest. Cell cycle arrest in G2/M and sub-G1 phases indicate potential anti-cancer properties of both Cdots and CNBs in melanoma cancer cells, respectively.

Generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) can play an important role for cellular toxicity. Additionally, ROS has been considered to be a key mediator of the programmed cell death pathway [43]. In this context, we measured the production of ROS (H2O2 or O2
−) quantitatively using spectrofluorimeter in both cancer cell (B16F10) and normal cell (NIH-3T3) treated with Cdots and CNBs.

Several published reports demonstrated that carbon dots or other carbon materials like graphene can generate various types of ROS [hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) or superoxide radicals (O2
−)] inside cells [36,44]. Based upon the spectrofluorimetric data, cancer cells (B16F10) treated with Cdots and CNBs shows ~5–7 fold increase of ROS (H2O2 and O2
−) formation compared to untreated cells (Fig. 8 .a–d). In case of normal cells (NIH-3T3), the generation of ROS is almost similar or slightly higher (<1 fold) in treated cells compared to untreated cells (Fig. 8.a–d). However, cancer cells treated with Cdots and CNBs show more ROS production compared to normal cells treated with Cdots and CNBs. The results together demonstrate the biocompatibility of Cdots and CNBs to normal cells and their cytotoxicity towards cancer cells. These results also corroborate with higher uptake of Cdots and CNBs to cancer cells than normal cells. Thus we hypothesized that the cancer cell selective toxicity is probably due to the cancer cell specific internalization of Cdots and CNBs. However, the reason behind the cancer cell specific uptake of Cdots/CNBs is unclear and needs rigorous experimental study to understand the exact mechanism.

Caspase 9 initiates the apoptosis process in cells. Hence, Caspase 9 activity assay is a basic assay to check the extent of apoptosis induced by any anticancer drugs or nanomaterials. Accordingly we performed caspase-9 assay using B16F10 cell lysates treated with Cdots and CNBs (Fig. 9 ). Results show around 0.3 fold and 2.8 fold increase in the caspase-9 activity in the B16F10 cells treated with Cdots and CNBs; respectively compared to untreated B16F10 cells. The increase in the caspapse-9 activity in the treatment of Cdots and CNBs supports the anticancer activity of both the nanomaterials. Moreover, the cancer cell killing by Cdots and CNBs has probably gone through intrinsic (mitochondrial mediated) apoptosis pathway that initiates activation of the caspase cascade by caspase 9.

The difference in the biological response (fluorescence imaging and anti-cancer activity) of Cdots and CNBs are reasonably evident and might occur due to several reasons. In general, the main factors affecting the biological properties of nanomaterials includes (i) the size of the nanomaterials, (ii) the nature of dispersity, (iii) shape and surface area of the nanomaterials and (iv) doses of treatments [45]. However, in this case, the main factor probably influences the biological responses of Cdots and CNBs are the dispersity and nature of solubility of both the nanomaterials. Cdots are highly water soluble and dispersible in nature. On the other hand, CNBs are not nicely dispersible in water and needs prolonged sonication to achieve medium dispersity. Hence, CNBs nanoparticles are more aggregated in nature compared to Cdots. Consequently, CNBs show negligible fluorescence compared to Cdots as the fluorescence of CNBs was quenched on aggregations. Furthermore, the less dispersed and more aggregated CNBs may produce severe cell membrane damage and stress during the cellular internalization process and generate significant level of reactive oxygen species (ROS) as observed in Fig. 8. However, nicely dispersible and water soluble Cdots can easily internalized inside the cells and produce comparatively lesser ROS than CNBs. Importantly, carbon nanomaterials are prone to generate ROS and cause oxidative stress inside cells, which is the main reason for the cytotoxicity. Considering all the above issues, we predict that the difference in the biological activities of Cdots and CNBs are independent of size, shape and dose of treatments. Moreover, the differences in the biological responses probably occur due to the difference in the dispersion of the nanomaterials in the in vitro conditions. However, detailed studies are required to fully confirm about the structural relationship and physical properties of these nanomaterials with their biological activities which is currently out of scope of this present article.

The present work demonstrates a greener, eco-friendly and cost-effective synthetic method for the simultaneous production of carbon dots (Cdots) and carbon nanobeads (CNBs) from a naturally occurring bio-polymer Gum olibanum. The sensing of heavy metal cations of Cd2+, Pb2+ and Hg2+ was achieved by fluorescence quenching due to their complex interaction with the carboxyl group of Cdots. Each cation showed a different sensing profile of its own in its respective fluorescence spectra. Although this can be correlated with specificity, this study is just a preliminary study. Further investigation into the method of fluorescence quenching, specificity in sensing by anchoring moieties onto the Cdots will be carried out in the near future. This method could be extended to test the concentrations of various ions in water bodies like rivers and lake. The Cdots and CNBs are biocompatible to normal cells but show potent cytotoxicity to cancer cells as observed by various in vitro studies (cell viability assay, cell cycle assay, apoptosis assay, ROS determination assay, caspase-9 activity assay). Moreover, the fluorescence properties of Cdots were exploited for cell-imaging purposes. Hence, both Cdots and CNBs could be utilized as potent cancer theranostics agent. Importantly, the cancer specific cell killing is probably due to the cancer cell specific uptake of both the nanomaterials compared to normal cell lines. Furthermore, CNBs shows better therapeutic activity compared to Cdots (in lesser dose), which is probably due to the nature of dispersity of the nanomaterials. Based on the initial studies, the results altogether suggest that Cdots demonstrate multifunctional activities (sensor, cellular imaging and cancer therapy) that can be utilized towards various biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to gratefully acknowledge Intelcoat project (CSC-0114), Council of Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR, New Delhi, India) for a grant in aid and IICT-Hyderabad to carry out this work. CRP is grateful to DST, New Delhi for ‘Ramanujan Fellowship grants’ (SR/S2/RJN-04/2010; GAP0305) and CSIR, New Delhi for generous financial support (CSC0111 and CSC0302). SM is thankful to CSIR, New Delhi for his research fellowship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2016.12.095.
